headline: River Herring Population Surges to Highest Level in Decades
meta_description: Maine's river herring population has rebounded dramatically to over 20 million, marking the highest count in decades after dam removal restored river ecosystem…
summary_short: Maine's beloved river herring have made a spectacular comeback, with over 20 million counted during the annual spawning run—the highest number in decades.
body:
A quiet conservation success is unfolding in Maine's rivers. The river herring, a fish species that was prohibited from harvest as recently as 2012, has staged a remarkable recovery. This year's annual count topped 20 million fish, marking the highest number recorded in recent decades.

The turnaround traces directly to the removal of large dams that once blocked river systems. On the Penobscot River, the demolition of two major dams has been hailed as what many call the premier environmental success story of the era. By restoring natural river flows, engineers and conservationists opened pathways that alewife fish—the local name for river herring—had been unable to navigate for generations.

The recovery demonstrates the power of undoing old barriers to restore ecosystems. As these silver fish once again fill Maine's waterways during their spring spawning run, they bring not only ecological balance but also hope for the region's traditional fisheries and the communities that depend on them.
